summary.brief: Davies v. MultiCare Health Sys.. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: On August 23, 2017, Mari Davies was in a single-car rollover accident.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Davies’ stroke was caused by a vertebral artery dissection (VAD) that occurred at the time of the accident.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Emergency responders transported her to the emergency room (E.R.) at Good Samaritan Hospital in Puyallup.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When Davies arrived at the E.R. she had hypertension, high blood pressure, left shoulder pain, neck pain, chest pain, abdominal pain, a headache, and some tingling in her left arm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Michael Hirsig evaluated her as soon as she arrived in the E.R.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Hirsig ordered computerized tomography (CT) scans of her head, cervical spine, abdomen, chest, and pelvis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Davies’ CT scan showed a cervical spine fracture at the C3 level.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Morris recommended a cervical collar for eight weeks with a follow-up CT scan to check for healing and alignment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Davies visited her primary care provider, Dr. Larsen, the next day. While in his office, Davies exhibited stroke symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is undisputed that Dr. Hirsig did not order a CTA scan for Davies while she was in the E.R.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Davies’ experts testified that if Davies had undergone a CTA, it would have revealed her VAD and she would have received treatment that likely would have prevented the stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Davies' expert

evidence_index.text: experts testified that a VAD is 'commonly found' when there is a cervical spine fracture, so it is standard to perform a CTA scan if there is a C3 fracture.
evidence_index.speaker: Davies' expert

evidence_index.text: The trial court dismissed the informed consent claim on summary judgment; the jury found for the health care provider defendants on Davies’ negligence claim at trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We reverse the Court of Appeals and reinstate the trial court’s order dismissing Davies’ informed consent claim.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: A provider may not be liable for informed consent claims arising from a ruled out diagnosis under RCW 7.70.050.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Multiple injuries following a single-car rollover accident
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was involved in a single-car rollover accident and presented with hypertension, high blood pressure, left shoulder pain, neck pain, chest pain, abdominal pain, headache, and tingling in the left arm.

vital_signs.blood_pressure: Hypertension noted, exact values not documented

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Multiple trauma following motor vehicle accident

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scans of head, cervical spine, abdomen, chest, and pelvis

reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates potential for vertebral artery dissection
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of further imaging to rule out VAD

requestables.name: CTA Scan
requestables.description: Computed tomography angiography to detect vertebral artery dissection

reveal.clinical_significance: Would have detected vertebral artery dissection
reveal.decision_impact: Could have led to treatment preventing stroke

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to E.R. following a rollover accident

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup
timeline_phases.description: CT scans and other diagnostics performed

timeline_phases.name: Clinical Decision
timeline_phases.description: Decision made not to order CTA scan

key_events.event: Decision not to order CTA scan

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in E.R. following a rollover accident with a cervical spine fracture
decision_points.prompt: Should a CTA scan be ordered to rule out vertebral artery dissection?

options.description: Order CTA scan to rule out vertebral artery dissection
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to rule out VAD in presence of cervical spine fracture
Defendant ruled out VAD based on clinical presentation

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not order CTA scan
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant ruled out VAD based on lack of symptoms

expected_action_court.description: CTA scan should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated CTA is standard with cervical spine fracture

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order CTA scan led to missed diagnosis of VAD
explanation.what_should_have_happened: CTA scan would have detected VAD, allowing for preventive treatment
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered a stroke due to undiagnosed VAD

key_findings: Jury found no negligence by healthcare providers

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: CTA would have shown vertebral artery dissection

factual_timeline.event: Patient involved in a single-car rollover accident
Patient arrives at E.R. with multiple injuries

court_findings.legal_significance: Jury found no breach of standard of care
